payload.formula_text:
Ad Count per Strength = COUNT(DISTINCT AD_ID) WHERE AD_STRENGTH = [EXCELLENT | GOOD | AVERAGE | POOR]
Spend per Strength = SUM(COST_MICROS / 1,000,000)
payload.formula_explanation: Visualizes Google Ads quality distribution by ad strength rating. Ad strength is Google's quality indicator based on headlines, descriptions, and asset variety. This reveals how many ads fall into each quality tier and their associated spend.
how_to_read: The donut chart segments represent spend allocation by ad strength category
Green segments (Excellent/Good) indicate high-quality ads following best practices
Orange (Average) and red (Poor) segments highlight improvement opportunities
The center displays total spend across all ad strength categories
CTR metrics below each category show engagement correlation with quality
decisions_answered: What percentage of our Google ads have strong quality ratings?
How much budget is going to low-quality ads that could be improved?
Is there a correlation between ad strength and click-through rate?
Which ads should be prioritized for headline and description optimization?
payload.narration_text:
This donut chart displays your Google Ads quality distribution by ad strength rating.

Ad strength is Google's quality indicator based on asset variety, headline quality, and description relevance. The formula counts distinct ads per strength category and sums their associated spend.

Green segments show Excellent and Good rated ads. Orange represents Average. Red indicates Poor ratings. The center shows your total spend.

This helps answer: What percentage of ads have strong ratings? How much budget goes to low-quality ads? And which ads need optimization attention?

payload.formula_text:
Avg CTR per Day = AVG(CTR) for all ads at day N since launch
Day N = ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY AD_ID ORDER BY DATE)
Fatigue Threshold = 20% CTR decline from Day 1 baseline
payload.formula_explanation: Tracks CTR degradation over time to detect creative fatigue. Ads typically experience declining engagement as audiences become saturated. This curve helps identify the optimal refresh timing before performance deteriorates significantly.
how_to_read: Lines show average CTR for ads at each day since their launch
Blue line represents Google Ads, purple line shows Meta Ads
Downward slopes indicate fatigue setting in over time
Steeper drops suggest faster audience saturation
First 30 days are shown as this is the critical fatigue window
decisions_answered: How quickly do our creatives experience fatigue?
What is the optimal creative refresh cadence for our campaigns?
Do Google and Meta ads fatigue at different rates?
At what point should we rotate in fresh creative assets?
payload.narration_text:
This line chart tracks creative fatigue by showing CTR over time since ad launch.

The formula calculates average CTR for all ads at each day since launch using row numbering to track ad age. A 20% CTR decline signals the fatigue threshold.

Blue shows Google Ads performance, purple shows Meta. Downward slopes indicate fatigue. Steeper drops mean faster saturation.

This answers critical questions: How quickly do creatives fatigue? What's the optimal refresh timing? And do platforms fatigue differently?

payload.formula_text:
Google Spend = SUM(COST_MICROS / 1,000,000) per TYPE
Meta Spend = SUM(SPEND) per OBJECT_TYPE
CTR = (Clicks / Impressions) x 100
payload.formula_explanation: This chart aggregates spend and performance by creative type across both Google Ads (RSA, Shopping, Display, Video) and Meta Ads (Share, Video, Photo). It reveals which creative formats receive the most investment and generate the best engagement.
how_to_read: Horizontal bars represent total spend for each creative type
Google types (blue) and Meta types (purple) are grouped separately
Longer bars indicate higher investment in that creative format
Compare bar lengths to identify budget allocation patterns across formats
Hover over bars to see exact spend values and related metrics
decisions_answered: Which creative types receive the most budget allocation?
How does spend distribution differ between Google and Meta platforms?
Should we shift budget from underperforming formats to winning ones?
Are we diversifying our creative mix adequately across formats?
payload.narration_text:
This chart shows your spend distribution across creative types for both Google and Meta platforms.

The formula aggregates spend by creative type: Google spend from ad stats divided by one million to convert from micros, and Meta spend summed directly.

Horizontal bars display total investment per type. Blue represents Google formats like RSA and Shopping. Purple shows Meta formats like Share and Video.

Use this to answer: Which types get most budget? How does allocation differ between platforms? And should you shift investment to better-performing formats?

payload.formula_text:
Spend per CTA = SUM(SPEND) WHERE CALL_TO_ACTION_TYPE = [SHOP_NOW | LEARN_MORE | ORDER_NOW | etc.]
CTR = AVG(CTR) per CTA type
CPC = Spend / Clicks
payload.formula_explanation: Compares performance of different Meta Ads call-to-action buttons. CTAs signal user intent and affect both click quality and conversion likelihood. This analysis reveals which CTAs drive the most engagement and efficiency.
how_to_read: Horizontal bars show total spend for each CTA button type
SHOP_NOW typically dominates for e-commerce brands
CTR percentages below indicate engagement rate per CTA type
Compare spend allocation with CTR to identify efficiency opportunities
High spend with low CTR may indicate CTA mismatch with audience intent
decisions_answered: Which call-to-action buttons drive the highest engagement?
Is our CTA selection aligned with campaign objectives?
Should we test different CTAs on high-spend, low-CTR ads?
What CTA types work best for our specific audience segments?
payload.narration_text:
This chart analyzes Meta Ads call-to-action button performance.

The formula sums spend and averages CTR by CTA type, covering buttons like Shop Now, Learn More, and Order Now. Different CTAs signal different user intents.

Horizontal bars display spend per CTA. The CTR values below show engagement rates. Compare these to spot efficiency gaps.

Use this to answer: Which CTAs drive highest engagement? Are we using the right CTAs for our goals? And should we A/B test different buttons on underperforming ads?

payload.formula_text:
Open % = (Opens / Sends) x 100
Click % = (Clicks / Sends) x 100
CTOR = (Clicks / Opens) x 100
payload.formula_explanation: Shows top Klaviyo email campaigns ranked by sends, with engagement metrics. CTOR (Click-to-Open Rate) measures how compelling the email content is for those who actually opened it.
how_to_read: Campaigns are sorted by total sends (highest first)
Open % shows what fraction of recipients opened the email
Click % shows clicks as a percentage of total sends
CTOR (Click-to-Open Rate) measures clicks relative to opens â€” a better indicator of content quality
Use search to find specific campaigns by subject line
decisions_answered: Which email campaigns reached the most recipients?
Which subject lines drove the highest open rates?
Which email content generated the most clicks?
How does engagement vary across different campaign types?
payload.narration_text:
This table shows your top Klaviyo email campaigns ranked by sends, with engagement metrics.

Open percentage is opens divided by sends. Click percentage is clicks divided by sends. CTOR, or click-to-open rate, measures clicks relative to opens â€” a better indicator of content quality since it only counts engaged recipients.

Use this to identify which subject lines drive opens, which content drives clicks, and how engagement varies across campaigns.

payload.formula_text:
Media Type = CASE WHEN VIDEO_ID IS NOT NULL THEN "Video" WHEN IMAGE_HASH IS NOT NULL THEN "Image" ELSE "Other"
Spend per Type = SUM(SPEND)
CTR = AVG(CTR)
CPC = Spend / Clicks
payload.formula_explanation: Compares performance between image and video creative formats on Meta. This analysis helps determine whether your audience responds better to static or motion creative, informing content strategy and production priorities.
how_to_read: Cards display spend share percentage for each media type
CTR values show engagement rate per media type
Bar chart compares absolute spend between Image, Video, and Other
Higher CTR with proportional spend indicates efficient format
Large spend gaps may suggest format testing opportunities
decisions_answered: Does our audience respond better to image or video ads?
Should we invest more in video production or static images?
What is the efficiency difference between media types?
Are we allocating budget appropriately between formats?
payload.narration_text:
This chart compares image versus video creative performance on Meta.

The formula categorizes creatives by checking for video ID or image hash, then aggregates spend and CTR per type. This reveals which format resonates with your audience.

Cards show spend share percentages. CTR indicates engagement per type. The bar chart displays absolute spend comparison.

Use this to answer: Does your audience prefer images or video? Should you invest more in video production? And is your budget allocation optimal between formats?

payload.formula_text:
Monthly Spend = SUM(COST_MICROS / 1,000,000) per TYPE per MONTH
Month = DATE_TRUNC("month", DATE)
payload.formula_explanation: Shows how spend allocation across creative types has evolved over the past year. This long-term view reveals seasonal patterns, strategic shifts, and trends in creative format investment.
how_to_read: Each line represents a different creative type over 12 months
Upward trends indicate increasing investment in that format
Crossing lines show changing format priorities over time
Seasonal patterns may appear around key shopping periods
Sudden shifts might indicate strategic pivots or market changes
decisions_answered: How has our creative mix evolved over the past year?
Are there seasonal patterns in creative type performance?
Which formats are gaining or losing share over time?
Do our spend trends align with strategic priorities?
payload.narration_text:
This line chart tracks creative type spending trends over 12 months.

The formula aggregates spend by creative type and month, using date truncation to group by calendar month. This reveals long-term patterns.

Each line represents a creative type. Upward slopes mean increasing investment. Crossing lines show shifting priorities. Watch for seasonal patterns around holidays.

Use this to understand: How has your creative mix evolved? Are there seasonal patterns? Which formats are gaining share? And do trends match your strategy?

payload.formula_text:
CTR = (Clicks / Impressions) x 100
ROAS = Conversion Value / Spend
CPC = Spend / Clicks
Days Active = COUNT(DISTINCT DATE)
payload.formula_explanation: Ranks individual ads by spend to reveal your highest-investment creatives and their performance. Combines Google Ads and Meta Ads in tabbed views with comprehensive metrics for quick comparison and optimization decisions.
how_to_read: Tabs switch between Google Ads and Meta Ads performance tables
Rows are sorted by spend to show highest-investment ads first
ROAS color coding: green (3x+), yellow (2-3x), red (below 2x)
Use search and column sorting to find specific ads or metrics
Click rows to drill into individual ad-level analytics
decisions_answered: Which individual ads receive the most budget?
Are our highest-spend ads also our highest-ROAS ads?
Which ads should be scaled up or paused based on performance?
How do performance metrics vary between Google and Meta ads?
payload.narration_text:
This table ranks your top ads by spend across Google and Meta platforms.

Key formulas: CTR is clicks over impressions times 100. ROAS is conversion value divided by spend. Days Active counts unique active dates.

Switch tabs to compare platforms. Rows are sorted by spend. ROAS is color-coded: green for 3x plus, yellow for 2 to 3x, red for below 2x.

This answers: Which ads get most budget? Are high-spend ads also high-ROAS? And which should you scale or pause?
